http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TelevisionEp.label: television episode
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TelevisionEp.description: A television episode is a part of serial television program.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Egyptologist.label: egyptologist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Egyptologist.description: Egyptology is the scientific study of ancient Egypt, including historic artifacts and ancient writing.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CelestialBod.label: celestial body
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CelestialBod.description: Celestial Body is a 2000 Croatian film directed by Lukas Nola.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/AustralianFo.label: australian football league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/AustralianFo.description: A group of sports teams that compete against each other in australian football.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Star.label: star
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Star.description: A star is a luminous spheroid of plasma held together by self-gravity.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VicePrimeMin.label: vice prime minister
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VicePrimeMin.description: A deputy prime minister or vice prime minister is, in some countries, a government minister who can take the position of acting prime minister when the prime minister is temporarily absent.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Referee.label: referee
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Referee.description: An official who watches a game or match closely to ensure that the rules are adhered to.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Arachnid.label: arachnid
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Arachnid.description: Arachnids are arthropods in the class Arachnida of the subphylum Chelicerata.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Glacier.label: glacier
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Glacier.description:
A glacier is a persistent body of natural ice, a form of
rock, that is constantly moving under its own weight.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Scientist.label: scientist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Scientist.description: A scientist is an expert who conducts scientific research to advance knowledge in science.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CricketLeagu.label: cricket league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CricketLeagu.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Cricket

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ChristianDoc.label: Christian Doctrine
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ChristianDoc.description: Tenets of the Christian faith, e.g. Trinity, Nicene Creed

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Monument.label: monument
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Monument.description: A type of structure (a statue or an art object) created to commemorate a person or important event, not necessarily of a catastrophic nature.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sculptor.label: sculptor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sculptor.description: Sculpture is the branch of the visual arts that operates in three dimensions.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsEvent.label: sports event
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsEvent.description: a event of competitive physical activity

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SoccerLeague.label: soccer league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SoccerLeague.description: A group of sports teams that compete against each other in soccer.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tax.label: tax
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tax.description: A tax is a mandatory financial charge or levy imposed on an individual or legal entity by a governmental organization to support government spending and public expenditures collectively or to regulate economic activity through measures designed to mitigate negative externalities.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TrackList.label: track list
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TrackList.description: A list of music tracks, like on a CD

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Judge.label: judge
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Judge.description: A judge is a person who presides over court proceedings, either alone or as a part of a judicial panel.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mill.label: Mill
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mill.description: a unit operation designed to break a solid material into smaller pieces

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/book.label: name
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/book.description: A book is a written work of substantial length created by one or more authors.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PopulatedPla.label: populated place
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PopulatedPla.description: In geography, statistics and archaeology, a settlement, locality or populated place is a community of people living in a particular place.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Nebula.label: Nebula
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Nebula.description: A nebula is a distinct luminescent part of interstellar medium, which can consist of ionized, neutral, or molecular hydrogen and also cosmic dust.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Non-ProfitOr.label: non-profit organisation
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Non-ProfitOr.description: A nonprofit organization (NPO), also known as a nonbusiness entity, nonprofit institution, not-for-profit organization (NFPO), or simply nonprofit, is a non-governmental entity that operates for a collective, public, or social benefit, rather than to generate profit for private owners.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ArtificialSa.label: ArtificialSatellite
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ArtificialSa.description: In the context of spaceflight, an artificial satellite is an artificial object which has been intentionally placed into orbit.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CardGame.label: card game
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CardGame.description: A card game is any game that uses cards as the primary device with which the game is played, whether the cards are of a traditional design or specifically created for the game (proprietary).

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Rocket.label: rocket
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Rocket.description: A rocket is an elongated flying vehicle that uses a rocket engine to accelerate without using any surrounding air.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TopLevelDoma.label: top level domain
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TopLevelDoma.description: A top-level domain (TLD) is one of the domains at the highest level in the hierarchical Domain Name System of the Internet after the root domain.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RoadJunction.label: road junction
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RoadJunction.description: A road junction is a type of junction where two or more roads meet.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mine.label: mine
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mine.description: A mine is a place where mineral resources are or were extracted

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sales.label: sales
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sales.description: Sales are activities related to selling or the number of goods sold in a given targeted time period.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SpaceShuttle.label: space shuttle
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SpaceShuttle.description: The Space Shuttle is a retired, partially reusable low Earth orbital spacecraft system operated from 1981 to 2011 by the U.S.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Weapon.label: weapon
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Weapon.description: A weapon, arm, or armament is any implement or device that is used to deter, threaten, inflict physical damage, harm, or kill.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Conifer.label: conifer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Conifer.description: Conifers are a group of vascular plants and a subset of gymnosperms.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RailwayLine.label: railway line
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RailwayLine.description: A railway line is a transport service by trains that pull passengers or freight provided by an organization. Not to be mistaken for railway track, which is the structure consisting of the rails. Wikipedia do not clearly differentiate between both, so there is one infobox describing tracks and lines.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Reference.label: Reference
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Reference.description: Reference to a work (book, movie, website)  providing info about the subject

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Airline.label: airline
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Airline.description: An airline is a company that provides a regular service of air transportation for passengers or freight (cargo).

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Archeologist.label: archeologist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Archeologist.description: Archaeology or archeology is the study of human activity through the recovery and analysis of material culture.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TeamMember.label: Team member
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TeamMember.description: A member of an athletic team.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/FormulaOneRa.label: formula one racing
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/FormulaOneRa.description: A Formula One race weekend is an auto racing event which takes place over three days, with a series of practice and qualifying sessions prior to the race on Sunday.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Moss.label: moss
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Moss.description: Mosses are small, non-vascular flowerless plants in the taxonomic division Bryophyta sensu stricto.

http://schema.org/Festival.label: Festival
http://schema.org/Festival.description: A festival is an event celebrated by a community and centering on some characteristics aspect or aspects of that community and its religion or cultures.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BusinessPers.label: businessperson
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BusinessPers.description: The term entrepreneur mainly means someone who holds a senior position, such as an executive.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Ginkgo.label: ginkgo
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Ginkgo.description: Ginkgo is a genus of non-flowering seed plants, assigned to the gymnosperms.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TennisLeague.label: tennis league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TennisLeague.description: A group of sports teams or person that compete against each other in tennis.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MetroStation.label: subway station
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MetroStation.description: A metro station or subway station is a train station for a rapid transit system, which as a whole is usually called a "metro" or "subway".

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Psychologist.label: psychologist
aliases: Training and licensing of clinical psychologists
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Psychologist.description: A psychologist is a professional who practices psychology and studies mental states, perceptual, cognitive, emotional, and social processes and behavior.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Governmental.label: governmental administrative region
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Governmental.description: An administrative body governing some territorial unity, in this case a governmental administrative body

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Building.label: building
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Building.description: A building or edifice is an enclosed structure with a roof, walls and often windows, usually standing permanently in one place, such as a house or factory.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Demographics.label: demographics
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Demographics.description: Population of a place. Uses these properties: populationTotal, year (when measured, populationYear), rank (sortOrder of this place amongst its siblings at the same level), name (areal measured by the population, eg: "locality", "municipality" or "comitat")

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cat.label: cat
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cat.description: The cat, also called domestic cat and house cat, is a small carnivorous mammal.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/NobleFamily.label: Noble family
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/NobleFamily.description: Family deemed to be of noble descent

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tournament.label: tournament
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tournament.description: A tournament is a competition involving at least three competitors, all participating in a sport or game.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Restaurant.label: restaurant
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Restaurant.description: A restaurant is a business that prepares and serves food and beverages to customers.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Media.label: media
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Media.description: storage and transmission channels or tools used to store and deliver information or data

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bridge.label: bridge
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bridge.description: A bridge is a structure designed to span an obstacle, such as a river or railroad, allowing vehicles, pedestrians, and other loads to pass across.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Stream.label: stream
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Stream.description: a flowing body of water with a current, confined within a bed and stream banks

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RadioHost.label: radio host
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RadioHost.description: A radio personality is a person who has an on-air position in radio broadcasting.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Embryology.label: embryology
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Embryology.description: Embryology is the branch of zoology that studies the prenatal development of gametes sex cells, fertilization and development of embryos and fetuses.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/WikimediaTem.label: Wikimedia template
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/WikimediaTem.description: DO NOT USE THIS CLASS! This is for internal use only!

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MountainPass.label: mountain pass
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MountainPass.description: a path that allows the crossing of a mountain chain. It is usually a saddle point in between two areas of higher elevation

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GrossDomesti.label: gross domestic product
aliases: Lists of countries by GDP per capita
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GrossDomesti.description: Gross domestic product (GDP) is a monetary measure of the total market value of all of the final goods and services which are produced and rendered during a specific period of time by a country or countries.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalRe.label: Historical region
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalRe.description: a place which used to be a region.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Arena.label: arena
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Arena.description: An arena is a large enclosed venue, often circular or oval-shaped, designed to showcase theatre, musical performances or sporting events.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Aircraft.label: aircraft
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Aircraft.description: An aircraft is a vehicle that is able to fly by gaining support from the air.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MilitaryServ.label: military service
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MilitaryServ.description: Military service is service by an individual or group in an army or other militia, air forces, and naval forces, whether as a chosen job (volunteer) or as a result of an involuntary draft...

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Company.label: company
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Company.description: A company is a legal entity representing an association of legal persons with a shared objective, such as generating profit or benefiting society.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Linguist.label: linguist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Linguist.description: Linguistics is the scientific study of language.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GridironFoot.label: gridiron football player
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GridironFoot.description: Gradiron football, also called North American football, is a family of soccer team games played primarily in the United States and Canada.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Crustacean.label: crustacean
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Crustacean.description: Crustaceans are mandibulate arthropods that are traditionally a part of the paraphyletic subphylum Crustacea.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Eukaryote.label: eukaryote
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Eukaryote.description: The eukaryotes are the domain of Eukaryota or Eukarya, organisms whose cells have a membrane-bound nucleus.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sculpture.label: Sculpture
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sculpture.description: Sculpture is three-dimensional artwork created by shaping or combining hard materials, typically stone such as marble, metal, glass, or wood, or plastic materials such as clay, textiles, polymers and softer metals.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/UndergroundJ.label: underground journal
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/UndergroundJ.description: An underground journal is, although over time there have always been publications forbidden by law, a phenomenon typical of countries occupied by the Germans during the Second World War. The writing in the underground press aims at stiffening a spirit of resistance against Nazi occupation. The distribution of underground journals had to be very secretive and was, therefore, very much dependant on illegal distribution circuits and the hazards of persecution by the occupant.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Algorithm.label: Algorithm
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Algorithm.description: In mathematics and computer science, an algorithm is a finite sequence of mathematically rigorous instructions, typically used to solve a class of specific problems or to perform a computation.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sea.label: sea
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sea.description: A sea is a large body of salt water.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Synagogue.label: synagogue
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Synagogue.description: A synagogue, sometimes spelt synagog, is a Jewish or Samaritan house of prayer.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VideogamesLe.label: videogames league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VideogamesLe.description: A group of sports teams or person that compete against each other in videogames.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Horse.label: horse
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Horse.description: The horse is a domesticated, one-toed, hoofed mammal.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lipid.label: lipid
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lipid.description: Lipids are a broad group of organic compounds that include fats, waxes, sterols, fat-soluble vitamins, monoglycerides, diglycerides, phospholipids, and others.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Country.label: country
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Country.description: A country is an area of land with defined borders and government that operates as a sovereign state.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SiteOfSpecia.label: Site of Special Scientific Interest
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SiteOfSpecia.description: A Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) is a conservation designation denoting a protected area in the United Kingdom. SSSIs are the basic building block of site-based nature conservation legislation and most other legal nature/geological conservation designations in Great Britain are based upon them, including National Nature Reserves, Ramsar Sites, Special Protection Areas, and Special Areas of Conservation.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BrownDwarf.label: brown dwarf
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BrownDwarf.description: Brown dwarfs are substellar objects that have more mass than the biggest gas giant planets, but less than the least massive main-sequence stars.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Reptile.label: reptile
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Reptile.description: Reptiles, as commonly defined, are tetrapod vertebrate animals with an ectothermic metabolism and amniotic development.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mayor.label: mayor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mayor.description: In many countries, a mayor is the highest-ranking official in a municipal government such as that of a city or a town.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/EmployersOrg.label: Employers' Organisation
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/EmployersOrg.description: An employers' organisation is an organisation of entrepreneurs who work together to coordinate their actions in the field of labour relations

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Crater.label: crater
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Crater.description: A crater is a landform consisting of a hole or depression on a planetary surface, usually caused either by an object hitting the surface, or by geological activity on the planet.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Writer.label: writer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Writer.description: A writer is a person who uses written words in different writing styles, genres, and techniques to communicate ideas, to inspire feelings and emotions, or to entertain.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bodybuilder.label: bodybuilder
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bodybuilder.description: Bodybuilding is the practice of progressive resistance exercise to build, control, and develop one's muscles via hypertrophy.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Reign.label: reign
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Reign.description: A reign is the period of a person's or dynasty's occupation of the office of monarch of a nation, of a people or of a spiritual community.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Election.label: Election
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Election.description: An election is a formal group decision-making process whereby a portion or all of a population or group votes to choose an individual or multiple individuals to hold public office or other position of...

http://schema.org/Organization.label: Organization
http://schema.org/Organization.description: An organization or organisation is an entity—such as a company, or corporation or an institution, or an association—comprising one or more people and having a particular...

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Drug.label: drug
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Drug.description: A drug is any chemical substance other than a nutrient or an essential dietary ingredient, which, when administered to a living organism, produces a biological effect.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Automobile.label: automobile
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Automobile.description: A car, or an automobile, is a motor vehicle with wheels.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CountrySeat.label: country estate
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CountrySeat.description: A country seat is a rural patch of land owned by a land owner.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Poet.label: poet
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Poet.description: A poet is a person who studies and creates poetry.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VicePresiden.label: vice president
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VicePresiden.description: A vice president or vice-president, also director in British English, is an officer in government or business who is below the president in rank.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BroadcastNet.label: broadcast network
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BroadcastNet.description: A broadcast network is a group of radio stations, television stations, or other electronic media outlets, that form an agreement to air, or broadcast, content from a centralized source.

http://schema.org/MusicRecording.label: MusicRecording
http://schema.org/MusicRecording.description: Sound recording and reproduction is the electrical, mechanical, electronic, or digital inscription and re-creation of sound waves, such as spoken voice, singing, instrumental music, or sound effects.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Province.label: province
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Province.description: An administrative body governing a territorial unity on the intermediate level, between local and national level

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tank.label: Tank
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tank.description: A tank is an armoured fighting vehicle intended as a primary offensive weapon in front-line ground combat.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Brewery.label: brewery
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Brewery.description: A brewery or brewing company is a business that makes and sells beer.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Historian.label: historian
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Historian.description: A historian is a scholar who studies and writes about the past and is regarded as an authority on it.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Wrestler.label: wrestler
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Wrestler.description: Wrestling is a martial art, combat sport, and form of entertainment that involves grappling with an opponent and striving to obtain a position of advantage through different throws or techniques, within a given ruleset.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Motorcycle.label: motorcycle
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Motorcycle.description: A motorcycle is a two or three-wheeled motor vehicle steered by a handlebar from a saddle-style seat.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/LacrosseLeag.label: lacrosse league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/LacrosseLeag.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Lacrosse.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GolfCourse.label: golf course
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GolfCourse.description: In a golf course, holes often carry hazards, defined as special areas to which additional rules of the game apply.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CardinalDire.label: Cardinal direction
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CardinalDire.description: One of the four main directions on a compass or any other system to determine a geographical position

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PublicServic.label: public service
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PublicServic.description: A public service or service of general (economic) interest is any service intended to address the needs of aggregate members of a community, whether provided directly by a public sector agency, via public financing available to private businesses or voluntary organisations, or by private businesses...

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Parish.label: parish
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Parish.description: The smallest unit of a clerical administrative body

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SnookerWorld.label: snooker world ranking
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SnookerWorld.description: The official world ranking in snooker for a certain year/season

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Philosopher.label: philosopher
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Philosopher.description: Philosophy is a systematic study of general and fundamental questions concerning topics like existence, knowledge, mind, reason, language, and value.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ClericalAdmi.label: clerical administrative region
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ClericalAdmi.description: An administrative body governing some territorial unity, in this case a clerical administrative body

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Economist.label: economist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Economist.description: An economist is a professional in the social science discipline of economics.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/FieldHockeyL.label: field hockey league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/FieldHockeyL.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Field Hockey

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Comic.label: comic
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Comic.description: Comics is a medium used to express ideas with images, often combined with text or other visual information.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/UnitOfWork.label: unit of work
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/UnitOfWork.description: This class is meant to convey the notion of an amount work to be done. It is different from Activity in that it has a definite end and is being measured.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tram.label: streetcar
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tram.description: A tram is an urban rail transit type in which vehicles, whether individual railcars or multiple-unit trains, run on tramway tracks on urban public streets; some include segments on segregated right-of-way.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BowlingLeagu.label: bowling league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BowlingLeagu.description: a group of sports teams or players that compete against each other in Bowling

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/AutoRacingLe.label: auto racing league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/AutoRacingLe.description: a group of sports teams or individual athletes that compete against each other in auto racing

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RomanEmperor.label: roman emperor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RomanEmperor.description: The Roman emperor was the ruler and monarchical head of state of the Roman Empire, starting with the granting of the title augustus to Octavian in 27 BC.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Skier.label: skier
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Skier.description: Skiing is the use of skis to glide on snow for basic transport, a recreational activity, or a competitive winter sport.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cape.label: cape
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cape.description: A cape is a clothing accessory or a sleeveless outer garment of any length that hangs loosely and connects either at the neck or shoulders.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/NationalAnth.label: National anthem
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/NationalAnth.description: Patriotic musical composition which is the offcial national song.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CapitalOfReg.label: Capital of region
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CapitalOfReg.description: seat of a first order administration division.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Garden.label: garden
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Garden.description: A garden is a planned space, usually outdoors, set aside for the cultivation, display, and enjoyment of plants and other forms of nature.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Song.label: song
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Song.description: A song is a musical composition performed by the human voice.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Caterer.label: Caterer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Caterer.description: Catering is the business of providing food and beverages at a variety of venues, including private residences, banquet halls, special event spaces, hotels, business parks, festivals, film locations/studios, and other locations.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Amphibian.label: amphibian
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Amphibian.description: Amphibians are ectothermic, anamniotic, four-limbed vertebrate animals that constitute the class Amphibia.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Galaxy.label: galaxy
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Galaxy.description: A galaxy is a system of stars, stellar remnants, interstellar gas, dust, and dark matter bound together by gravity.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Concentratio.label: Concentration camp
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Concentratio.description: Internment is the imprisonment of people, commonly in large groups, without charges or intent to file charges.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Meeting.label: meeting
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Meeting.description: A regular or irregular meeting of people as an event to keep record of

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Profession.label: profession
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Profession.description: A profession is a field of work that has been formally professionalized.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bay.label: bay
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bay.description: A bay is a recessed, coastal body of water that directly connects to a larger main body of water, such as an ocean, a lake, or another bay.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Hospital.label: hospital
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Hospital.description: A hospital is a healthcare institution providing patient treatment with specialized medical science and auxiliary healthcare staff and medical equipment.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TelevisionDi.label: Television director
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TelevisionDi.description: a person who directs the activities involved in making a television program.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Forest.label: forest
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Forest.description: A natural place more or less densely grown with trees

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cyclist.label: cyclist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cyclist.description: Cycling, also known as bicycling or biking, is the activity of riding a bicycle or other types of pedal-driven human-powered vehicles such as balance bikes, unicycles, tricycles, and quadricycles.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Robot.label: Robot
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Robot.description: A robot is a machine, especially one programmable via a computer, capable of automatically carrying out a complex series of actions.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cave.label: cave
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cave.description: Caves and caverns are natural voids under the surface of the Earth and have also been observed in other rocky worlds.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Senator.label: senator
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Senator.description: A senate is a deliberative assembly, often the upper house or chamber of a bicameral legislature.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Chancellor.label: chancellor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Chancellor.description: Chancellor is a title of various official positions in the governments of many countries.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RallyDriver.label: rally driver
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RallyDriver.description: Rally Driver is a racing and driving simulation game developed by Five Ways Software and published by Hill MacGibbon and Alternative Software.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Disease.label: disease
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Disease.description: A disease is a particular abnormal condition that adversely affects the structure or function of all or part of an organism and is not immediately due to any external injury.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Flag.label: flag
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Flag.description: A flag is a piece of fabric with distinctive colours and design, often flown from a pole and used for symbolic, signalling, or decorative purposes.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Governor.label: governor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Governor.description: A governor is an administrative leader and head of a polity or political region, in some cases, such as governors-general, as the head of a state's official representative.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Station.label: station
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Station.description: Public transport station (eg. railway station, metro station, bus station).

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Annotation.label: Annotation
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Annotation.description: An annotation is extra information associated with a particular point in a document or other piece of information.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RailwayTunne.label: railway tunnel
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RailwayTunne.description: A tunnel is an underground or undersea passageway.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BritishRoyal.label: British royalty
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BritishRoyal.description: The British royal family comprises King Charles III and other members of his family.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PoloLeague.label: polo league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PoloLeague.description: A group of sports teams that compete against each other in Polo.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SpaceMission.label: space mission
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SpaceMission.description: Spaceflight is an application of astronautics to fly objects, usually spacecraft, into or through outer space, either with or without humans on board.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Territory.label: territory
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Territory.description: A territory may refer to a country subdivision, a non-sovereign geographic region.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GolfTourname.label: golf tournament
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GolfTourname.description: Golf is a club-and-ball sport in which players use various clubs to hit a ball into a series of holes on a course in as few strokes as possible.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ScientificCo.label: Scientific concept
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ScientificCo.description: Scientific concepts, e.g. Theory of relativity, Quantum gravity

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Prefecture.label: prefecture
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Prefecture.description: A prefecture is an administrative jurisdiction traditionally governed by an appointed prefect.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TelevisionSh.label: television show
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TelevisionSh.description: A television show, TV program, or simply a TV show, is the general reference to any content produced for viewing on a television set that is transmitted via over-the-air, satellite, and cable, or distributed digitally on streaming platforms.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Politician.label: politician
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Politician.description: A politician is a person who participates in policy-making processes, usually holding a position in a political party or an elective position in government.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ResearchProj.label: research project
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ResearchProj.description: A research project is a scientific investigation, usually using scientific methods, to achieve defined objectives.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Dike.label: dike
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Dike.description: A dike is an elongated naturally occurring ridge or artificially constructed fill or wall, which regulates water levels

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TradeUnion.label: trade union
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TradeUnion.description: A trade union or labor union is an organization of workers who have banded together to achieve common goals such as better working conditions.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Gnetophytes.label: Gnetophytes
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Gnetophytes.description: Gnetophyta is a division of plants, grouped within the gymnosperms, that consists of some 70 species across the three relict genera: Gnetum, Welwitschia, and Ephedra.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sailor.label: Sailor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sailor.description: A sailor, seaman, mariner, or seafarer is a person who works aboard a watercraft as part of its crew, and may work in any one of a number of different fields that are related to the operation and maintenance of a ship.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SpaceStation.label: space station
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SpaceStation.description: A space station is a spacecraft which remains in orbit and hosts humans for extended periods of time.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsTeam.label: sports team
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsTeam.description: Sports Team are an English alternative rock band based in London.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MusicFestiva.label: music festival
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MusicFestiva.description: A music festival is a community event with performances of singing and instrument playing that is often presented with a theme such as musical genre, musical instrument, nationality, locality of musicians, or holiday.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SystemOfLaw.label: System of law
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SystemOfLaw.description: a system of legislation, either national or international

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Newspaper.label: newspaper
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Newspaper.description: A newspaper is a regularly scheduled publication containing news of current events, informative articles, diverse features and advertising. It usually is printed on relatively inexpensive, low-grade paper such as newsprint.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Genre.label: genre
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Genre.description: Genre is any style or form of communication in any mode with socially agreed-upon conventions developed over time.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Project.label: project
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Project.description: A project is a temporary endeavor undertaken to achieve defined objectives.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Website.label: website
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Website.description: A website, or web site, is any web page whose content is identified by a common domain name and is published on at least one web server.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lymph.label: lymph
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lymph.description: Lymph is the fluid that flows through the lymphatic system, a system composed of lymph vessels (channels) and intervening lymph nodes whose function, like the venous system, is to return fluid from the tissues to be recirculated.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/District.label: district
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/District.description: A district is a type of administrative division that in some countries is managed by the local government.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Journalist.label: journalist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Journalist.description: A journalist is a person who gathers information in the form of text, audio or pictures, processes it into a newsworthy form and disseminates it to the public.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ProgrammingL.label: programming language
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ProgrammingL.description: A programming language is an engineered language for expressing computer programs, typically allowing software to be written in a human readable manner.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Wine.label: wine
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Wine.description: Wine is an alcoholic drink made from fermented grape juice.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Theatre.label: theatre
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Theatre.description: A theater or theatre (also a playhouse) is a structure where theatrical works or plays are performed or other performances such as musical concerts may be produced.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sport.label: sport
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sport.description: A sport is commonly defined as an organized, competitive, and skillful physical activity.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GolfLeague.label: golf league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GolfLeague.description: Golfplayer that compete against each other in Golf

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Administrati.label: administrative region
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Administrati.description: A PopulatedPlace under the jurisdiction of an administrative body. This body may administer either a whole region or one or more adjacent Settlements (town administration)
children: ancient area of jurisdiction of a person (feudal) or of a governmental body

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Deanery.label: deanery
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Deanery.description: The intermediate level of a clerical administrative body between parish and diocese

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Congressman.label: congressman
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Congressman.description: A member of congress (MOC), also known as a congressman, congresswoman or congressperson is a person who has been appointed or elected and inducted into an official body called a congress, typically to represent a particular constituency in a legislature.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Priest.label: priest
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Priest.description: A priest is a religious leader authorized to perform the sacred rituals of a religion, especially as a mediatory agent between humans and one or more deities.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Youtuber.label: Youtuber
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Youtuber.description: a person who uploads, produces, or appears in videos on the video-sharing website YouTube..

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Outbreak.label: Outbreak
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Outbreak.description: In epidemiology, an outbreak is a sudden increase in occurrences of a disease when cases are in excess of normal expectancy for the location or season.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tower.label: tower
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tower.description: A Tower is a kind of structure (not necessarily a building) that is higher than the rest

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RestArea.label: rest area
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RestArea.description: A rest area is part of a Road, meant to stop and rest. More often than not, there is a filling station

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Capital.label: Capital
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Capital.description: A municipality enjoying primary status in a state, country, province, or other region as its seat of government.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Church.label: church
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Church.description: This is used for church buildings, not any other meaning of church.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Painting.label: Painting
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Painting.description: Describes a painting to assign picture entries in wikipedia to artists.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/NaturalPlace.label: natural place
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/NaturalPlace.description: The natural place encompasses all places occurring naturally in universe.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PlayWright.label: Playwright
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PlayWright.description: A person who writes dramatic literature or drama.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Year.label: year
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Year.description: A year is a unit of time based on how long it takes the Earth to orbit the Sun.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ArtisticGenr.label: artistic genre
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ArtisticGenr.description: Genres of art, e.g. Pointillist, Modernist

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Law.label: law
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Law.description: Law is a set of rules that are created and enforced by governmental or societal institutions to regulate behavior, with its precise definition a matter of longstanding debate.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Shrine.label: shrine
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Shrine.description: A shrine is a sacred space dedicated to a specific deity, ancestor, hero, martyr, saint, daemon, or similar figure of respect, wherein they are venerated or worshipped.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Gene.label: gene
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Gene.description: In biology, the word gene has two meanings.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Agent.label: agent
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Agent.description: Analogous to a foaf:Agent, an agent is an entity that acts. This is intended to be the super class of Person and Organisation.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Astronaut.label: astronaut
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Astronaut.description: An astronaut is a person trained, equipped, and deployed by a human spaceflight program to serve as a commander or crew member of a spacecraft.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SocietalEven.label: societal event
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SocietalEven.description: an event that is clearly different from strictly personal events

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Swarm.label: Swarm
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Swarm.description: Swarm behaviour, or swarming, is a collective behaviour exhibited by entities, particularly animals, of similar size which aggregate together, perhaps milling about the same spot or perhaps moving en masse or migrating in some direction.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RadioStation.label: radio station
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RadioStation.description: A radio station has one line up. For instance the radio station BBC Radio 1. Not to be confused with the broadcasting network BBC, which has many radio stations.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ProtectedAre.label: protected area
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ProtectedAre.description: This class should be used for protected nature. For enclosed neighbourhoods there is now class GatedCommunity

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sound.label: sound
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Sound.description: Sound is a phenomenon in which pressure disturbances propagate through an elastic material medium.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Street.label: street
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Street.description: A Street is different from a Road in as far as the infrastructure aspect is much less important here. A Street is a social and architectural ensemble much more than the connection between two geographic points.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RugbyLeague.label: rugby league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RugbyLeague.description: A group of sports teams that compete against each other in rugby.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CyclingLeagu.label: cycling league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CyclingLeagu.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Cycling

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CurlingLeagu.label: curling league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CurlingLeagu.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Curling

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Humorist.label: humorist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Humorist.description: A humorist is an intellectual who uses irony, or wit in writing and speaking.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Ship.label: ship
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Ship.description: A ship is a large watercraft designed for travel across the surface of a body of water, carrying cargo or passengers, or in support of specialized tasks such as warfare, oceanography and fishing.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Altitude.label: altitude
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Altitude.description: Altitude is a distance measurement, usually in the vertical or "up" direction, between a reference datum and a point or object.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Insect.label: insect
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Insect.description: Insects are hexapod invertebrates of the class Insecta.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsTeamMe.label: Sports team member
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsTeamMe.description: A member of an athletic team.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Fern.label: fern
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Fern.description: The ferns are a group of vascular plants that reproduce via spores and have neither seeds nor flowers.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Murderer.label: murderer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Murderer.description: Murder is the unlawful killing of another human without justification or valid excuse and committed with the necessary intent as defined by the law in a specific jurisdiction.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Valley.label: valley
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Valley.description: a depression with predominant extent in one direction

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CareerStatio.label: career station
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CareerStatio.description: this class marks a career step in the life of a person, e.g. a soccer player, holding information on the time span, matches and goals he or she achieved at a certain club

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Port.label: Port
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Port.description: a location on a coast or shore containing one or more harbors where ships can dock and transfer people or cargo to or from land.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Grape.label: grape
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Grape.description: A grape is a fruit, botanically a berry, of the deciduous woody vines of the flowering plant genus Vitis.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/City.label: city
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/City.description: a relatively large and permanent settlement, particularly a large urban settlement

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Swimmer.label: swimmer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Swimmer.description: a trained athlete who participates in swimming meets

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Archbishop.label: archbishop
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Archbishop.description: In Christianity, an archbishop is a bishop of higher rank or office.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Hotel.label: hotel
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Hotel.description: A hotel is an establishment that provides paid lodging on a short-term basis.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/StatedResolu.label: Stated Resolution
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/StatedResolu.description: A Resolution describes a formal statement adopted by a meeting or convention.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Deity.label: deity
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Deity.description: A deity or god is a supernatural being considered to have authority over some aspect of the universe and/or life, with many also being considered as sacred and worthy of worship.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Language.label: language
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Language.description: Language is a structured system of communication that consists of grammar and vocabulary.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Vodka.label: vodka
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Vodka.description: Vodka is a clear distilled alcoholic beverage.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VaccinationS.label: Vaccination Statistics
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VaccinationS.description: Statistics related to the COVID-19 vaccination world progress‎

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RadioControl.label: radio-controlled racing league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RadioControl.description: A group of sports teams or person that compete against each other in radio-controlled racing.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Family.label: family
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Family.description: A group of people related by common descent, a lineage.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Treadmill.label: Treadmill
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Treadmill.description: A mill driven by the tractive power of horses, donkeys or even people

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Artist.label: artist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Artist.description: An artist is a person who creates art, or practices the arts.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Square.label: square
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Square.description: In geometry, a square is a regular quadrilateral.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VideoGame.label: video game
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VideoGame.description: A video game is an electronic game that involves interaction with a user interface to generate visual feedback on a video device.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Temple.label: temple
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Temple.description: A temple is a place of worship, a building used for spiritual rituals and activities such as prayer and sacrifice.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Satellite.label: Satellite
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Satellite.description: A satellite or an artificial satellite is an object, typically a spacecraft, placed into orbit around a celestial body.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Artwork.label: artwork
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Artwork.description: A work of art, artwork, art piece, or art object is an aesthetic item or artistic creation.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/College.label: college
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/College.description: A college may be a tertiary educational institution, part of a collegiate university, an institution offering vocational education, a further education institution, or a secondary...

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Chef.label: chef
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Chef.description: a person who cooks professionally for other people

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Volcano.label: volcano
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Volcano.description: A volcano is currently subclass of naturalplace, but it might also be considered a mountain.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MilitaryStru.label: military structure
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MilitaryStru.description: A military structure such as a Castle, Fortress, Wall, etc.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Museum.label: museum
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Museum.description: A museum is an institution dedicated to displaying and preserving culturally or scientifically significant objects.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Park.label: park
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Park.description: A park is an area of natural, semi-natural or planted space set aside for human enjoyment and recreation or for the protection of wildlife or natural habitats.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Community.label: Community
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Community.description: A community is a group of living organisms, people, plants or animals that live in a common environment.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Racecourse.label: racecourse
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Racecourse.description: A racecourse is an alternate term for a horse racing track, found in countries such as the United Kingdom, Australia, Hong Kong, and the United Arab Emirates.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Saint.label: saint
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Saint.description: In Christian belief, a saint, also known as a hallow, is a person who is recognized as having an exceptional degree of holiness, likeness, or closeness to God.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GovernmentCa.label: cabinet of ministers
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GovernmentCa.description: A cabinet is a body of high-ranking state officials, typically consisting of the top leaders of the executive branch.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bank.label: bank
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bank.description: a company which main services are banking or financial services.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Woman.label: woman
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Woman.description: A woman is an adult female human.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HandballLeag.label: handball league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HandballLeag.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Handball

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Employer.label: Employer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Employer.description: a person, business, firm, etc, that employs workers.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Dog.label: dog
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Dog.description: The dog is a domesticated descendant of wolves.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Protein.label: protein
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Protein.description: Proteins are large biomolecules and macromolecules that comprise one or more long chains of amino acid residues.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Polysacchari.label: polysaccharide
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Polysacchari.description: Polysaccharides are "Compounds consisting of a large number of monosaccharides linked glycosidically".

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Dam.label: dam
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Dam.description: A dam is part of a landscape infrastructure, like waterworks (canals) or roads, much more than a building, though, of course, it has been built, too.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Ocean.label: Ocean
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Ocean.description: A body of saline water that composes much of a planet's hydrosphere.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Memorial.label: memorial
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Memorial.description: A monument erected to commemorate a person, an event and/or group. In the case of a person, this might be a grave or tomb.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GraveMonumen.label: grave stone or grave monument
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GraveMonumen.description: A monument erected on a tomb, or a memorial stone.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Desert.label: Desert
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Desert.description: Barren land where there is less rain.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Locomotive.label: locomotive
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Locomotive.description: A locomotive is a rail vehicle that provides the motive power for a train.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Single.label: single
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Single.description: In music, a single or record single is a type of release, typically a recording of fewer tracks than an LP or a CD.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Treaty.label: treaty
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Treaty.description: A treaty is a recorded international agreement between sovereign states or other subjects of international law that is governed by international law.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Island.label: island
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Island.description: An island or isle is a piece of land, distinct from a continent, completely surrounded by water.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/School.label: school
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/School.description: A school is an educational institution designed to provide learning environments for the teaching of students, usually under the direction of teachers.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Spacecraft.label: spacecraft
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Spacecraft.description: A spacecraft is a vehicle that is designed to fly and operate in outer space.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Anime.label: Anime
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Anime.description: A style of animation originating in Japan

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CultivatedVa.label: cultivar (cultivated variety)
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CultivatedVa.description: A cultivar is a plant or grouping of plants selected for desirable characteristics that can be maintained by propagation. A plant whose origin or selection is primarily due to intentional human activity.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cleric.label: cleric
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cleric.description: Clergy are leaders within established religions.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Book.label: book
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Book.description: A book is a written work of substantial length created by one or more authors.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Professor.label: professor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Professor.description: Professor is an academic rank at universities and other post-secondary education and research institutions in most countries.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Beer.label: beer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Beer.description: Beer is an alcoholic beverage produced by the brewing and fermentation of starches from cereal grain—most commonly malted barley, although wheat, maize, rice, and oats are also used.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Award.label: award
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Award.description: An award, sometimes called a distinction, is given to a recipient as a token of recognition of excellence in a certain field.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Enzyme.label: enzyme
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Enzyme.description: An enzyme is a biological macromolecule, usually a protein, that acts as a biological catalyst, accelerating chemical reactions without being consumed in the process.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Philosophica.label: Philosophical concept
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Philosophica.description: Philosophical concepts, e.g. Existentialism, Cogito Ergo Sum

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Beverage.label: beverage
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Beverage.description: A drink, or beverage, is a liquid which is specifically prepared for human consumption.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/FormerMunici.label: one-time municipality
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/FormerMunici.description: A municipality that has ceased to exist, and most of the time got incorporated (wholesale or partly) into another municipality

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/LiteraryGenr.label: literary genre
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/LiteraryGenr.description: Genres of literature, e.g. Satire, Gothic

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Guitarist.label: guitarist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Guitarist.description: A guitarist is a person who plays the guitar.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/WorldHeritag.label: World Heritage Site
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/WorldHeritag.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site is a site (such as a forest, mountain, lake, desert, monument, building, complex, or city) that is on the list that is maintained by the international World Heritage Programme administered by the UNESCO World Heritage Committee, composed of 21 state parties which are elected by their General Assembly for a four-year term. A World Heritage Site is a place of either cultural or physical significance.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Animal.label: animal
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Animal.description: Animals are multicellular, eukaryotic organisms belonging to the biological kingdom Animalia.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SnookerPlaye.label: snooker player
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SnookerPlaye.description: An athlete that plays snooker, which is a billard derivate

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/WrittenWork.label: written work
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/WrittenWork.description: Written work is any text written to read it (e.g.: books, newspaper, articles)

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BasketballLe.label: basketball league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BasketballLe.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Basketball

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Internationa.label: international organisation
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Internationa.description: An international organisation is either a private or a public organisation seeking to accomplish goals across country borders

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PersonalEven.label: personal event
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PersonalEven.description: an event that occurs in someone's personal life

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalAr.label: ancient area of jurisdiction of a person (feudal) or of a governmental body
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalAr.description: Mostly for feudal forms of authority, but can also serve for historical forms of centralised authority

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Archaea.label: archaea
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Archaea.description: Archaea is a domain of organisms.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cheese.label: cheese
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cheese.description: A milk product prepared for human consumption

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BaseballLeag.label: baseball league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BaseballLeag.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Baseball.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Work.label: work
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Work.description: Item on which time has been spent for its realisation. Actor can be a human on not (machine, insects, nature...)

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cipher.label: Cipher
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cipher.description: In cryptography, a cipher is an algorithm for performing encryption or decryption—a series of well-defined steps that can be followed as a procedure.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Identifier.label: identifier
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Identifier.description: An identifier is a name that identifies either a unique class of objects or a unique instance of such objects; the class or instance may be an idea, person, physical countable object, or physical noncountable substance.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Decoration.label: decoration
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Decoration.description: An object, such as a medal or an order, that is awarded to honor the recipient ostentatiously.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bone.label: bone
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bone.description: A bone is a rigid organ that constitutes part of the skeleton in most vertebrate animals.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Fashion.label: fashion
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Fashion.description: type or code of dressing, according to the standards of the time or individual design.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Poem.label: poem
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Poem.description: Poetry is a form of literary art that uses aesthetic and often rhythmic qualities of language to evoke meanings in addition to, or in place of, literal or surface-level meanings.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Village.label: village
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Village.description: a clustered human settlement or community, usually smaller a town

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsTeamSe.label: sports team season
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsTeamSe.description: A season for a particular sports team (as opposed to the season for the entire league that the team is in)
children: national collegiate athletic association team season

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Ligament.label: ligament
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Ligament.description: A ligament is a type of fibrous connective tissue in the body that connects bones to other bones.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Jockey.label: jockey (horse racer)
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Jockey.description: A jockey is someone who rides horses in horse racing or steeplechase racing, primarily as a profession.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalSe.label: Historical settlement
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalSe.description: A place which used to be a city or town or village.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Colour.label: colour
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Colour.description: Color or colour is the visual perceptual property corresponding in humans to the categories called red, yellow, blue and others. Color derives from the spectrum of light (distribution of light energy versus wavelength) interacting in the eye with the spectral sensitivities of the light receptors.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MotorcycleRa.label: motorcycle racing league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MotorcycleRa.description: a group of sports teams or bikerider that compete against each other in Motorcycle Racing

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Database.label: Database
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Database.description: In computing, a database is an organized collection of data or a type of data store based on the use of a database management system (DBMS), the software that interacts with end users, applications, and the database itself to capture and analyze the data.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Medicine.label: Medicine
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Medicine.description: The science and art of healing the human body and identifying the causes of disease

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Gymnast.label: gymnast
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Gymnast.description: A gymnast is one who performs gymnastics

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TeamSport.label: team sport
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TeamSport.description: A team sport is commonly defined as a sport that is being played by competing teams

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PaintballLea.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Paintball

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/InformationA.description: An information device such as PDAs or Video game consoles, etc.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Architect.label: architect
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Architect.description: An architect is a person who plans, designs, and oversees the construction of buildings.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Monarch.label: monarch
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Monarch.description: A monarch is a head of state for life or until abdication, and therefore the head of state of a monarchy.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Actor.label: actor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Actor.description: An actor or actress is a person who acts in a dramatic production and who works in film, television, theatre, or radio in that capacity.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Pyramid.label: Pyramid
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Pyramid.description: a structure whose shape is roughly that of a pyramid in the geometric sense.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Baronet.label: baronet
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Baronet.description: A baronet or the female equivalent, a baronetess, is the holder of a baronetcy, a hereditary title awarded by the British Crown.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Plant.label: plant
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Plant.description: Plants are the eukaryotic organisms that constitute the kingdom Plantae.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Constellatio.label: constellation
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Constellatio.description: A constellation is an area on the celestial sphere in which a group of visible stars forms a perceived pattern or outline, typically representing an animal, mythological subject, or inanimate...

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalEv.label: historical event
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalEv.description: an event that is clearly different from strictly personal events and had historical impact

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BoxingLeague.label: boxing league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/BoxingLeague.description: A group of sports teams or fighters that compete against each other in Boxing

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Atoll.label: atoll
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Atoll.description: An atoll is a ring-shaped island, including a coral rim that encircles a lagoon.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Town.label: town
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Town.description: a settlement ranging from a few hundred to several thousand (occasionally hundreds of thousands). The precise meaning varies between countries and is not always a matter of legal definition. Usually, a town is thought of as larger than a village but smaller than a city, though there are exceptions to this rule.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Criminal.label: criminal
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Criminal.description: In ordinary language, a crime is an unlawful act punishable by a state or other authority.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Taxon.label: taxonomic group
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Taxon.description: a category within a classification system for Species

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Vicar.label: vicar
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Vicar.description: A vicar is a representative, deputy or substitute; anyone acting "in the person of" or agent for a superior.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CityDistrict.label: city district
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CityDistrict.description: District, borough, area or neighbourhood in a city or town

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Fort.label: fort
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Fort.description: Fortified place, most of the time to protect traffic routes

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Region.label: region
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Region.description: In geography, regions, otherwise referred to as areas, zones, lands or territories, are portions of the Earth's surface that are broadly divided by physical characteristics, human impact characteristics, and/or the interaction of humanity and the environment.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TheologicalC.label: Theological concept
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TheologicalC.description: Theological concepts, e.g. The apocalypse, Trinty, Stoicism

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Competition.label: competition
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Competition.description: Competition is a rivalry where two or more parties strive for a common goal which cannot be shared: where one's gain is the other's loss.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/AcademicSubj.label: academic subject
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/AcademicSubj.description: Genres of art, e.g. Mathematics, History, Philosophy, Medicine

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Model.label: model
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Model.description: A model is an informative representation of an object, person, or system.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Zoo.label: zoo
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Zoo.description: A zoo is a facility where animals are kept within enclosures for public exhibition and often bred for conservation purposes.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Rebellion.label: rebellion
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Rebellion.description: Rebellion is an uprising that resists and is organized against one's government.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mountain.label: mountain
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mountain.description: A mountain is an elevated portion of the surface of a planet, generally with steep sides that show significant exposed bedrock.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Instrumental.label: instrumentalist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Instrumental.description: A musical instrument is a device created or adapted to make musical sounds.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Game.label: game
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Game.description: a structured activity, usually undertaken for enjoyment and sometimes used as an educational tool

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Infrastructu.label: infrastructure
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Infrastructu.description: Infrastructure is the set of facilities and systems that serve a country, city, or other area, and encompasses the services and facilities necessary for its economy, households and firms to function.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalPe.label: historical period
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalPe.description: A historical Period should be linked to a Place by way of the property dct:spatial (already defined)

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Archipelago.label: archipelago
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Archipelago.description: An archipelago, sometimes called an island group or island chain, is a chain, cluster, or collection of islands.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Pilot.label: Pilot
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Pilot.description: An aircraft pilot, or aviator, is an individual who controls an aircraft's flight by operating its directional controls.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Olympics.label: olympics
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Olympics.description: The modern Olympic Games are the world's preeminent international sporting events.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Area.label: area
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Area.description: Area of something. Use "value" for the value, "min" & "max" for a range (if uncertain) and "rank" (integer) for the rank of that thing amongst its siblings (eg regions ordered by area)

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Biomolecule.label: Biomolecule
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Biomolecule.description: A biomolecule or biological molecule is loosely defined as a molecule produced by a living organism and essential to one or more typically biological processes.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Earthquake.label: earthquake
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Earthquake.description: the result of a sudden release of energy in the Earth's crust that creates seismic waves

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Engine.label: engine
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Engine.description: An engine or motor is a machine designed to convert one or more forms of energy into mechanical energy.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Pretender.label: Pretender
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Pretender.description: A pretender is someone who claims to be the rightful ruler of a country although not recognized as such by the current government.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Type.label: type
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Type.description: a category within a classification system

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Skyscraper.label: skyscraper
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Skyscraper.description: A skyscraper is a tall building with many habitable floors.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/LegalCase.label: Legal Case
children: Supreme Court of the United States case

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Producer.label: Producer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Producer.description: a person who manages movies or music recordings.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Film.label: movie
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Film.description: A film, movie, or motion picture is a work of visual art that simulates experiences and otherwise communicates ideas, stories, perceptions, emotions, or atmosphere through the use of moving images that are generally, since the 1930s, synchronized with sound and sometimes using other sensory...
