http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Regency.description: In a monarchy, a regent is a person appointed to execute the office of a monarch temporarily, essentially as a substitute ruler.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Road.label: road
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Road.description: A road is a thoroughfare from one place to another, primarily used for movement of traffic.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Magazine.label: magazine
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Magazine.description: Magazines, periodicals, glossies or serials are publications, generally published on a regular schedule, containing a variety of articles. They are generally financed by advertising, by a purchase price, by pre-paid magazine subscriptions, or all three.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Casino.label: casino
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Casino.description: In modern English, a casino is a facility which houses and accommodates certain types of gambling activities.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Aristocrat.label: aristocrat
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Aristocrat.description: The aristocracy is historically associated with a "hereditary" or a "ruling" social class.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TelevisionSt.label: television station
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TelevisionSt.description: A television station has usually one line up. For instance the television station WABC-TV (or ABC 7, Channel 7). Not to be confused with the broadcasting network ABC, which has many television stations.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SoapCharacte.label: soap character
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SoapCharacte.description: Character of soap - radio or television serial frequently characterized by melodrama, ensemble casts, and sentimentality.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Statistic.label: statistic
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Statistic.description: A statistic (singular) or sample statistic is any quantity computed from values in a sample which is considered for a statistical purpose.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Archive.label: Archive
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Archive.description: Collection of documents pertaining to a person or organisation.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Vein.label: vein
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Vein.description: A vein is a blood vessel in the circulatory system of humans and most other animals that carries blood towards the heart.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Continent.label: continent
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Continent.description: A continent is any of several large terrestrial geographical regions.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Manor.label: Manor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Manor.description: Estate and/or (cluster of) lands that are under the jurisdiction of a feudal lord. Hence it is also the shorthand expression for the physical estate itself: a manor is a stately house in the countryside with the surrounding grounds

ancestor_chain: ancient area of jurisdiction of a person (feudal) or of a governmental body

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MicroRegion.description: A microregion is a - mainy statistical - region in Brazil, at an administrative level between a meso-region and a community

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Brain.label: brain
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Brain.description: The brain is an organ that serves as the center of the nervous system in all vertebrate and most invertebrate animals.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Nerve.label: nerve
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Nerve.description: A nerve is an enclosed, cable-like bundle of nerve fibers.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/FootballMatc.label: football match
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/FootballMatc.description: a competition between two football teams

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Windmill.label: Windmill
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Windmill.description: A windmill is a machine that converts the energy of wind into rotational energy by means of vanes called sails

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VolleyballLe.label: volleyball league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/VolleyballLe.description: A group of sports teams that compete against each other in volleyball.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cartoon.label: cartoon
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cartoon.description: A cartoon is a type of visual art that is typically drawn, frequently animated, in an unrealistic or semi-realistic style.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/AmericanFoot.label: american football league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/AmericanFoot.description: A group of sports teams that compete against each other in american football.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Resume.label: Resume
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Resume.description: A Resume describes a persons work experience and skill set.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lawyer.label: Lawyer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lawyer.description: a person who is practicing law.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Canton.label: canton
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Canton.description: An administrative (France) or lawcourts (Netherlands) body governing a territorial unity on the municipal level or somewhat above

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Species.label: species
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Species.description: A category in the rating system

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mineral.label: mineral
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mineral.description: A naturally occurring solid chemical substance.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TheatreDirec.label: Theatre director
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/TheatreDirec.description: A director in the theatre field who oversees and orchestrates the mounting of a theatre production.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/AdultActor.label: adult (pornographic) actor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/AdultActor.description: A pornographic actor or actress or a porn star is a person who performs sex acts in film, normally characterised as a pornographic film.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Manhwa.label: manhwa
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Manhwa.description: Korean term for comics and print cartoons

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SubMunicipal.label: borough

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CoalPit.label: coal pit
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CoalPit.description: A coal pit is a place where charcoal is or was extracted

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Photographer.label: photographer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Photographer.description: A photographer is a person who makes photographs, most commonly using a camera.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SnookerChamp.label: snooker world champion
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SnookerChamp.description: An athlete that plays snooker and won the world championship at least once

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/WaterTower.description: a construction designed to store larger quantities of water at a place of some elevation in order to keep pressure on the water provision system

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Religious.label: religious
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Religious.description: Religion is a range of social-cultural systems, including designated behaviors and practices, ethics, morals, beliefs, worldviews, texts, sanctified places, prophecies, or organizations, that generally relate humanity to supernatural, transcendental, and spiritual elements—although there is no...

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/NCAATeamSeas.label: national collegiate athletic association team season

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Food.label: Food
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Food.description: Food is any eatable or drinkable substance that is normally consumed by humans.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PeriodicalLi.label: periodical literature
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/PeriodicalLi.description: Periodical literature (also called a periodical publication or simply a periodical) is a published work that appears in a new edition on a regular schedule. The most familiar examples are the newspaper, often published daily, or weekly; or the magazine, typically published weekly, monthly or as a quarterly. Other examples would be a newsletter, a literary journal or learned journal, or a yearbook.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lighthouse.label: lighthouse
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lighthouse.description: A lighthouse is a tower, building, or other type of physical structure designed to emit light from a system of lamps and lenses and to serve as a beacon for navigational aid for maritime pilots at sea or on inland...

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Legislature.label: legislature
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Legislature.description: A legislature is a deliberative assembly that holds the legal authority to make law and exercise political oversight within a political entity such as a state, nation, or city.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tenure.label: tenure
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tenure.description: Tenure is a type of academic appointment that offers its holder a semi-permanent position at an institution, protecting them from being fired or laid off for their personal beliefs and practices.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Watermill.label: Watermill
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Watermill.description: A watermill is a structure that uses a water wheel or turbine to drive a mechanical process such as flour, lumber or textile production, or metal shaping (rolling, grinding or wire drawing)

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Manga.label: manga
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Manga.description: Manga are comics created in Japan

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Diocese.label: diocese
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Diocese.description: District or see under the supervision of a bishop.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalPr.label: Historical province
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalPr.description: A place which used to be a province.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Camera.label: camera
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Camera.description: A camera is an instrument used to capture and store images and videos, either digitally via an electronic image sensor, or chemically via a light-sensitive material such as photographic film.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Attack.label: attack
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Attack.description: An Attack is not necessarily part of a Military Conflict

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/University.label: university
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/University.description: A university is an institution of tertiary education and research which awards academic degrees in several academic disciplines.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Hormone.label: Hormone
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Hormone.description: A hormone is any member of a class of signaling molecules produced by glands in multicellular organisms that are transported by the circulatory system to target distant organs to regulate physiology and behaviour.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Castle.label: castle
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Castle.description: Castles often are, but need not be a military structure. They can serve for status, pleasure and hunt as well.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mosque.label: mosque
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mosque.description: A mosque, sometimes spelt mosk, is a place of worship for followers of Islam.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mollusca.label: mollusca
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mollusca.description: Mollusca is a phylum of protostomic invertebrate animals, whose members are known as molluscs or mollusks.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MusicDirecto.label: music director
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MusicDirecto.description: A person who is the director of an orchestra or concert band.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ControlledDe.label: Controlled designation of origin wine
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ControlledDe.description: A quality assurance label for wines

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bacteria.label: bacteria
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bacteria.description: Bacteria are ubiquitous, mostly free-living organisms often consisting of one biological cell.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Currency.label: currency
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Currency.description: A currency is a standardized form of money, in use or circulation as a medium of exchange, for example banknotes, coins, electronic balances in online bank accounts, and central bank digital currencies (CBDCs).

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Fungus.label: fungus
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Fungus.description: A fungus is any member of the group of eukaryotic organisms that includes yeasts, molds, as well as mushrooms.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Spy.label: Spy
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Spy.description: Espionage, spying, or intelligence gathering, as a subfield of the intelligence field, is the act of obtaining secret, confidential, or in some way valuable information.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Diploma.label: diploma
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Diploma.description: A diploma is a document awarded by an educational institution testifying the recipient has graduated by successfully completing their courses of studies.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Man.label: man
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Man.description: A man is a male adult human.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lieutenant.label: lieutenant
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lieutenant.description: A lieutenant is a junior commissioned officer rank in the armed forces of many nations, as well as fire services, emergency medical services, security services and police...

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Pharaoh.label: Pharaoh
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Pharaoh.description: The Pharaoh is term that denotes monarch of ancient Egypt.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/LawFirm.label: law firm
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/LawFirm.description: A law firm is a business entity formed by one or more lawyers to engage in the practice of law. The primary service provided by a law firm is to advise clients (individuals or corporations) about their legal rights and responsibilities, and to represent their clients in civil or criminal cases, business transactions, and other matters in which legal advice and other assistance are sought.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tunnel.label: tunnel
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Tunnel.description: A tunnel is an underground or undersea passageway.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Monastery.label: monastery
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Monastery.description: Monastery denotes the building, or complex of buildings, comprising the domestic quarters and workplace(s) of monastics, whether monks or nuns, and whether living in community or alone (hermits). The monastery generally includes a place reserved for prayer which may be a chapel, church or temple, and may also serve as an oratory.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cycad.label: cycad
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cycad.description: Cycads —constituting the division Cycadophyta—are seed plants with a stout, woody cylindrical trunk with a crown of large, hard, stiff, evergreen and usually pinnate (feather-shaped) leaves.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MountainRang.label: mountain range
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MountainRang.description: a chain of mountains bordered by highlands or separated from other mountains by passes or valleys.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Library.label: library
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Library.description: A library is a collection of books, and possibly other materials and media, that is accessible for use by its members and members of allied institutions.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mathematical.label: Mathematical concept
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Mathematical.description: Mathematical concepts, e.g. Fibonacci numbers, Imaginary numbers, Symmetry

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Album.label: album
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Album.description: An album is a collection of audio recordings issued on a medium such as compact disc (CD), vinyl (record), or audio tape, or in digital format.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Battery.label: battery
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Battery.description: The battery (type) used as energy source in vehicles.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MixedMartial.label: mixed martial arts league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MixedMartial.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Mixed Martial Arts

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Fish.label: fish
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Fish.description: A fish is an aquatic, anamniotic, gill-bearing vertebrate animal with a tough cranium to protect the brain, but lacking limbs with digits.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/IceHockeyLea.label: ice hockey league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/IceHockeyLea.description: a group of sports teams that compete against each other in Ice Hockey.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Surname.label: surname
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Surname.description: In many societies, a surname, last family name, or first family name is the mostly hereditary portion of one's personal name that indicates one's family.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Asteroid.label: asteroid
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Asteroid.description: An asteroid is a minor planet—an object larger than a meteoroid that is neither a planet nor an identified comet—that orbits within the inner Solar System or is co-orbital with Jupiter.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cricketer.label: cricketer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Cricketer.description: Cricket is a bat-and-ball game that is played between two teams of eleven players on a field, at the centre of which is a 22-yard pitch with a wicket at each end, each comprising two bails balanced on three stumps.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Engineer.label: engineer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Engineer.description: An engineer is a practitioner of engineering.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RouteStop.label: route stop
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/RouteStop.description: designated place where vehicles stop for passengers to board or alight

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Biologist.label: biologist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Biologist.description: A biologist is a scientist who conducts research in biology.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsLeague.label: sports league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsLeague.description: A group of sports teams or individual athletes that compete against each other in a specific sport.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Pope.label: pope
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Pope.description: The pope is the bishop of Rome and the head of the worldwide Catholic Church.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalDi.label: Historical district
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/HistoricalDi.description: a place which used to be a district.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Muscle.label: muscle
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Muscle.description: Muscle is a specialised soft tissue, one of the four basic types of animal tissues.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Blazon.label: Blazon
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Blazon.description: In heraldry and heraldic vexillology, a blazon is a formal description of a coat of arms, flag or similar emblem, from which the reader can reconstruct an accurate image.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/WindMotor.label: Wind motor
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/WindMotor.description: A wind-driven turbine that adapts itself to wind direction and to wind-force. Is considered to be a class in its own, despite the wind as common factor with Windmill.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ReligiousOrg.label: religious organisation
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ReligiousOrg.description: Formal organisation or organised group of believers

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/InlineHockey.label: inline hockey league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/InlineHockey.description: group of sports teams that compete against each other in Inline Hockey.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GovernmentAg.label: government agency
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/GovernmentAg.description: A government agency is a permanent or semi-permanent organization in the machinery of government that is responsible for the oversight and administration of specific functions, such as an intelligence agency.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Case.label: case
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Case.description: A case is the total of work done to prepare for an administrative or business decision. As a rule, a case is reflected in a set of documents.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Planet.label: planet
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Planet.description: A planet is a large, rounded astronomical body that is generally required to be in orbit around a star, stellar remnant, or brown dwarf, and is not one itself.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Opera.label: opera
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Opera.description: Opera is a form of Western theatre in which music is a fundamental component and dramatic roles are taken by singers.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Name.label: name
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Name.description: A name is a term used for identification by an external observer.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Stadium.label: stadium
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Stadium.description: A stadium is a place or venue for (mostly) outdoor sports, concerts, or other events and consists of a field or stage completely or partially surrounded by a tiered structure designed to allow spectators to stand or sit and view the event.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Airport.label: airport
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Airport.description: An airport is an aerodrome with extended facilities, mostly for commercial air transport.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lake.label: lake
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Lake.description: A lake is often a naturally occurring, relatively large and fixed body of water on or near the Earth's surface.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Farmer.label: farmer
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Farmer.description: A farmer is a person engaged in agriculture, raising living organisms for food or raw materials.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MovieDirecto.label: Movie director
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/MovieDirecto.description: a person who oversees making of film.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Prison.label: prison
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Prison.description: A prison, also known as a jail, gaol, penitentiary, detention center, correction center, correctional facility, or remand center, is a facility where people are imprisoned under the authority of the state, usually as punishment for various crimes.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Spreadsheet.label: Spreadsheet
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Spreadsheet.description: A spreadsheet is a computer application for computation, organization, analysis and storage of data in tabular form.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CanadianFoot.label: liga de fútbol canadiense
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/CanadianFoot.description: A group of sports teams that compete against each other in canadian football league.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/LineOfFashio.label: line of fashion
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/LineOfFashio.description: A coherent type of clothing or dressing following a particular fashion

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Parliament.label: parliament
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Parliament.description: A parliament is a type of legislature, or law-making body, of a state.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Holiday.label: holiday
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Holiday.description: A holiday is a day or other period of time set aside for festivals or recreation.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsManage.label: sports manager
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SportsManage.description: According to the french label sub Soccer, trainership could be meant. However, here a Sportsmanager is interpreted as a member of the board of a sporting club.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Factory.label: factory
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Factory.description: A factory (previously manufactory) or manufacturing plant is an industrial site, usually consisting of buildings and machinery, or more commonly a complex having several buildings, where workers manufacture goods or operate machines processing one product into another.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Arrondisseme.label: arrondissement
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Arrondisseme.description: An administrative (France) or lawcourts (Netherlands) body governing a territorial unity on the intermediate level, between local and national level

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Organ.label: organ
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Organ.description: All types and sizes of organs

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Drama.label: drama
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Drama.description: Drama is the specific mode of fiction represented in performance: a play, opera, mime, ballet, etc., performed in a theatre, or on radio or television.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Software.label: software
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Software.description: Software consists of computer programs that instruct the execution of a computer.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Person.label: person
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Person.description: A person is a being who has certain capacities or attributes such as reason, morality, consciousness or self-consciousness, and being a part of a culturally established form of social relations such as kinship, ownership of property, or legal responsibility.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SpeedwayLeag.label: speedway league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SpeedwayLeag.description: A group of sports teams that compete against each other in motorcycle speedway racing.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Play.label: play
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Play.description: A play is a form of literature written by a playwright, usually consisting of scripted dialogue between characters, intended for theatrical performance rather than just reading.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/DistrictWate.label: district water board
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/DistrictWate.description: Conservancy, governmental agency dedicated to surface water management

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Train.label: train
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Train.description: A train is a series of connected vehicles that run along a railway track and transport people or freight.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SoftballLeag.label: softball league
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/SoftballLeag.description: A group of sports teams that compete against each other in softball.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Comedian.label: comedian
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Comedian.description: A comedian or comic is a person who seeks to entertain an audience by making them laugh.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Entomologist.label: entomologist
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Entomologist.description: Entomology, from Ancient Greek ἔντομον (éntomon), meaning "insect", and λόγος (lógos), meaning "study", is the branch of zoology that focuses on insects.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Beach.label: beach
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Beach.description: The shore of a body of water, especially when sandy or pebbly.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bird.label: bird
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Bird.description: Birds are a group of warm-blooded vertebrate animals constituting the class Aves, characterised by feathers, toothless beaked jaws, the laying of hard-shelled eggs, a high metabolic rate, a four-chambered heart, and a strong yet lightweight skeleton.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ElectricalSu.label: electrical substation
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/ElectricalSu.description: Substations transform voltage from high to low, or the reverse.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Painter.label: painter
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Painter.description: Painting is a visual art, characterized by the practice of applying paint, pigment, color or other medium to a solid surface.

http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Ambassador.label: ambassador
http://dbpedia.org/ontology/Ambassador.description: An ambassador is the highest ranking diplomat that represents a nation and is usually accredited to a foreign sovereign or government, or to an international organization.
